q: What do we call the force that pulls objects down toward the Earth?
explain: Gravity is the force that pulls objects toward the center of the Earth.

q: Which of these actions is an example of a pull?
explain: Opening a drawer toward your body means you are pulling it closer.

q: Why does a sled slide more easily on smooth ice than on rough grass?
explain: Smooth ice has less friction, so objects slide across it more easily.

q: What happens to a moving swing after you stop pushing it?
options: It flies off into the sky
It slowly slows down and stops
explain: Forces like friction and air resistance gradually slow the swing until it stops.

q: Which simple machine is a seesaw an example of?
explain: A seesaw is a lever that balances on a turning point called a fulcrum.

q: In a vacuum with no air, a heavy bowling ball and a light tennis ball are dropped together. Which lands first?
options: They land at the same time
explain: Without air resistance, gravity makes all objects fall at the same rate no matter their mass.

q: What does a ramp, also called an inclined plane, help you do?
options: Lift a load using less force over a longer distance
explain: A ramp lets you raise a load with less force by spreading the work over a longer distance.

q: Which of these gives your shoes the most friction on the floor?
explain: Rough, grippy rubber soles create more friction, which helps you avoid slipping.

q: According to Newton's first law, a moving object keeps moving at a steady speed unless what happens?
options: An unbalanced force acts on it
explain: Newton's first law says motion stays the same until an unbalanced force changes it.

q: Newton's third law says that for every action force there is an equal and opposite what?
explain: Every action force is paired with an equal reaction force pushing in the opposite direction.

q: If you double the force pushing on a cart while its mass stays the same, its acceleration will do what?
explain: Newton's second law shows acceleration grows with force, so doubling the force doubles the acceleration.

q: A skater pushes off a wall and glides backward. Which idea best explains this?
explain: The skater pushes the wall, and the wall pushes back with an equal and opposite force.

q: Which of these is a source of light and heat energy for Earth?
explain: The Sun gives off light and heat energy that travels all the way to Earth.

q: A stretched rubber band is holding what kind of stored energy?
explain: A stretched rubber band stores potential energy that is released when you let it go.

q: When you rub your hands together quickly, the energy of motion mostly turns into what?
explain: Rubbing changes motion energy into heat, which is why your hands feel warm.

q: Which of these gives your body the energy it needs to run and play?
explain: Food such as a banana stores energy your body can use to move and grow.

q: A ball resting at the top of a hill has the most of which kind of energy?
explain: Because of its high position, the ball stores gravitational potential energy.

q: As a roller coaster car races down a hill, its stored energy changes mostly into what?
explain: The car's potential energy at the top changes into kinetic energy as it speeds downhill.

q: How does heat move from a hot stove into a metal pan resting on it?
explain: Heat moves through direct contact between the stove and the pan by conduction.

q: Which energy change happens when a battery-powered flashlight is turned on?
explain: The battery's stored chemical energy is changed into electrical energy and then into light.

q: The law of conservation of energy states that energy can change form but cannot be what?
explain: Energy only changes from one form to another and is never created or destroyed.

q: Warm air rising while cooler air sinks to spread heat around a room is called what?
explain: Convection is the transfer of heat by the movement of a fluid such as air or water.

q: How does energy from the Sun travel through empty space to reach Earth?
explain: Radiation carries energy as waves that can travel through the vacuum of space.

q: In a swinging pendulum, energy keeps changing back and forth between which two forms?
explain: A pendulum trades kinetic energy at the bottom for potential energy at the top of each swing.

q: Which of these is a solid?
explain: A wooden block keeps its own shape, which is a property of solids.

q: What happens to water when it freezes?
options: It turns into a solid called ice
explain: When water gets cold enough, it freezes and becomes solid ice.

q: Which state of matter can be poured and takes the shape of its container?
explain: Liquids flow and take the shape of whatever container is holding them.

q: What do we call it when a puddle slowly dries up and turns into water vapor in the air?
explain: Evaporation is when liquid water changes into invisible water vapor gas.

q: When you mix sand and salt together, you make what?
options: A mixture that can be separated
explain: Sand and salt keep their own properties, so together they form a mixture you can separate.

q: Which of these is a chemical change?
explain: Burning wood makes new substances such as ash and smoke, which is a chemical change.

q: Why does a small metal coin sink while a large wooden log floats?
options: The coin is denser than water
explain: The coin is denser than water so it sinks, while the less dense wood floats.

q: When sugar fully dissolves in warm water, the sugar and water form a what?
explain: The sugar spreads evenly throughout the water to make a solution.

q: Which of these is a physical change rather than a chemical change?
explain: Bending copper changes its shape but not the substance, so it is a physical change.

q: Density describes the amount of mass packed into a certain what?
explain: Density compares how much mass is contained in a given volume of space.

q: When water boils and turns into steam, its molecules do what?
explain: Heating gives the molecules more energy, so they move faster and spread out as a gas.

q: Which statement about the particles in a solid is correct?
options: They are packed closely and vibrate in place
They move freely and fill any container
explain: Particles in a solid stay tightly packed and only vibrate in fixed positions.

q: What happens when you bring the two north poles of two magnets close together?
explain: Like poles, such as two norths, repel and push each other apart.

q: Which of these objects will a magnet stick to?
explain: Magnets are attracted to certain metals like iron.

q: Rubbing a balloon on your hair can make the balloon do what?
options: Attract your hair and make it stand up
explain: Rubbing builds up static electricity, which makes the balloon attract your hair.

q: What do we call the complete path that electricity flows through to light a bulb?
explain: Electricity flows in a complete loop called a circuit.

q: For a flashlight bulb to light up, the circuit must be what?
explain: Electricity can only flow when the circuit forms a complete, closed loop.

q: Which material is the best conductor of electricity?
explain: Metals like copper let electricity flow through them easily, so they are good conductors.

q: Why are electrical wires usually covered in rubber or plastic?
options: Those materials are insulators that block the current
explain: Rubber and plastic are insulators that keep the electricity safely inside the wire.

q: Where is a bar magnet's pull the strongest?
options: At its two ends, called poles
explain: A magnet's force is strongest at its poles, which are located at the two ends.

q: What surrounds a magnet and lets it attract objects without touching them?
explain: An invisible magnetic field surrounds a magnet and pulls on nearby magnetic objects.

q: How can you make a simple electromagnet?
options: Wrap a wire coil around an iron nail and connect it to a battery
Leave a nail out in the sun
explain: Electric current flowing through a wire coiled around iron creates an electromagnet.

q: In a series circuit with a single path, what happens to the other bulbs if one bulb burns out?
explain: A series circuit has one path, so a break at one bulb stops the current to all of them.

q: Static electricity builds up when a material gains or loses tiny charged particles called what?
explain: Static electricity comes from the transfer of negatively charged electrons between objects.

q: What forms when an object blocks light?
explain: A shadow forms in the space where an object blocks light from passing through.

q: Which object lets you see your reflection because it reflects light well?
explain: A smooth mirror reflects light clearly, so you can see your reflection in it.

q: What do you need in order to see the objects around you?
explain: We see objects when light bounces off them and travels into our eyes.

q: How is sound made?
explain: Sound is created when something vibrates and makes the air around it move.

q: Hitting a drum harder makes the sound do what?
explain: Hitting the drum harder makes bigger vibrations, which produce a louder sound.

q: A short, tight guitar string that vibrates very quickly makes a sound with what pitch?
explain: Faster vibrations create a higher pitched sound.

q: Why do you see the colors of a rainbow when sunlight shines through raindrops?
options: White light is separated into its colors
The Sun turns off for a moment
explain: Raindrops bend and split white sunlight into the separate colors that were already inside it.

q: Sound travels as waves and needs what in order to move from place to place?
options: A material like air, water, or a solid
explain: Sound waves need a medium such as air, water, or a solid to travel through.

q: When light passes from air into water and bends, this bending is called what?
explain: Refraction is the bending of light as it moves between materials of different densities.

q: A red apple looks red because it does what with light?
options: Absorbs red light and reflects the rest
Reflects red light and absorbs the other colors
explain: The apple reflects red light to our eyes while absorbing the other colors.

q: Why do you usually see a flash of lightning before you hear the thunder?
options: Light travels much faster than sound
explain: Light travels far faster than sound, so you see the lightning before the thunder reaches you.

q: Compared with traveling through air, sound usually travels through solids how?
explain: Particles are packed closer together in solids, so sound waves move faster through them than through air.

q: What do most plants need from the sun to help them grow?
explain: Plants use light energy from the sun to make their own food.

q: Which part of a plant usually holds it in the soil and soaks up water?
explain: Roots anchor a plant in the ground and take in water and nutrients from the soil.

q: What is a young frog called before it grows legs and leaves the water?
explain: A tadpole is the swimming baby stage of a frog before it becomes an adult.

q: Which of these animals is a mammal that feeds its babies milk?
explain: Dogs are mammals, and mammal mothers make milk to feed their young.

q: Butterflies go through a big body change during their life cycle called what?
explain: Metamorphosis is the change from egg to caterpillar to chrysalis to butterfly.

q: Which group of animals has feathers, wings, and lays eggs?
explain: Birds are the animal group known for feathers, wings, and laying eggs.

q: A cactus has thick stems and spiny leaves mainly to help it do what in the desert?
explain: A cactus stores water in its thick stem so it can survive where rain is rare.

q: What is the main job of a flower on a plant?
options: To help the plant breathe underwater
To help the plant make seeds
explain: Flowers help plants reproduce by making seeds that can grow into new plants.

q: Which word describes an animal that eats only plants?
explain: A herbivore is an animal whose diet is made up only of plants.

q: Scientists sort living things using classification; which is the broadest, largest group?
explain: A kingdom is the widest grouping, and it splits into smaller groups down to species.

q: A polar bear's thick fur and layer of fat are examples of what?
explain: Adaptations are body features that help an animal survive in its environment.

q: Which body part pumps blood all around your body?
explain: The heart is a muscle that pumps blood through your whole body.

q: How many senses do people traditionally have?
explain: The five traditional senses are sight, hearing, smell, taste, and touch.

q: Which body part do you use to smell things?
explain: Your nose senses smells from the air around you.

q: What covers and protects your body and is the largest organ you can see?
explain: Skin is your body's largest organ and it protects everything inside.

q: Which body system helps you breathe in oxygen and breathe out carbon dioxide?
explain: The respiratory system, including your lungs, moves gases in and out of your body.

q: What is one main job of your skeleton?
options: To give your body support and shape
To make you taller every day
explain: The bones of your skeleton hold you up and give your body its shape.

q: After you chew and swallow, where does most of your food get broken down?
explain: The stomach and intestines break food down so your body can use it.

q: Which organ controls your body and lets you think, move, and feel?
explain: The brain is the body's control center for thinking, moving, and sensing.

q: Red blood cells carry which gas from your lungs to the rest of your body?
explain: Red blood cells pick up oxygen in the lungs and deliver it around the body.

q: The nervous system sends messages using special cells called what?
explain: Neurons are nerve cells that carry signals throughout your body.

q: Which part of the digestive system absorbs most of the nutrients from food?
explain: The small intestine takes in most of the nutrients your food provides.

q: A place where an animal lives and finds food, water, and shelter is called its what?
explain: A habitat is the natural home where an animal gets everything it needs.

q: In a food chain, what is usually the main starting source of energy?
explain: The sun's energy is captured by plants, which begin most food chains.

q: Which of these is a living thing in an ecosystem?
explain: A rabbit is alive, while rocks, sunlight, and water are nonliving parts.

q: Bees moving pollen from flower to flower help plants do what?
explain: When bees carry pollen, they help flowers make seeds and fruit.

q: Animals that hunt and eat other animals for food are called what?
explain: Predators are animals that catch and eat other animals, called prey.

q: In a food web, which living things make their own food using sunlight?
explain: Producers, such as green plants, make their own food using sunlight.

q: What do decomposers like mushrooms and earthworms do in an ecosystem?
options: Break down dead plants and animals
explain: Decomposers recycle nutrients by breaking down things that have died.

q: If all the plants in a food chain disappeared, what would most likely happen to the plant-eaters?
options: They would have no food and struggle to survive
explain: Plant-eaters depend on plants for energy, so losing plants would leave them hungry.

q: What do we call all the different kinds of living things found in an area?
explain: Biodiversity means the variety of living things in a place.

q: When one type of living thing is suddenly removed, a food web can become what?
explain: Every living thing is connected, so removing one can throw the web out of balance.

q: Two animals that both need the same limited food are said to be in what with each other?
explain: Competition happens when living things must share a limited resource like food.

q: What is the smallest building block that makes up all living things?
explain: Cells are the tiny basic units that make up every living thing.

q: Traits like the color of your eyes are passed down from whom?
explain: You inherit traits like eye color from your parents.

q: Which of these is an inherited trait you were born with?
options: Knowing how to ride a bike
The natural color of your hair
explain: Natural hair color is inherited, while the others are things you learn.

q: A baby animal usually looks most similar to its what?
explain: Baby animals resemble their parents because they inherit their traits.

q: What is the control center of a cell that holds its instructions?
explain: The nucleus stores the cell's instructions and directs what the cell does.

q: The instructions inside your cells that decide your traits are made of what?
explain: DNA carries the coded instructions that shape your traits.

q: Which of these is a learned behavior rather than an inherited trait?
explain: Reading is learned through practice, while the others are inherited traits.

q: Plant cells have a stiff outer layer that animal cells do not have, called what?
explain: The cell wall gives plant cells extra support and structure.

q: Which part of the cell makes energy the cell can use, often called the powerhouse?
explain: Mitochondria release energy from food so the cell can work.

q: Genes are found on tiny thread-like structures inside the nucleus called what?
explain: Chromosomes are structures made of DNA that carry your genes.

q: Offspring often show a mix of traits from both parents because they receive what?
explain: Each parent passes on genes, so offspring blend traits from both.

q: What is falling from the sky when it rains, snows, sleets, or hails?
explain: Precipitation is water that falls from clouds as rain, snow, sleet, or hail.

q: Which of these do we see in the sky and is made of tiny water droplets?
explain: Clouds are made of many tiny water droplets floating in the air.

q: What do we call the day-to-day conditions outside, like sunny, rainy, or windy?
explain: Weather is what the air and sky are like outside right now.

q: The sun gives Earth which two important things?
explain: The sun provides Earth with light and heat that living things need.

q: In the water cycle, what is it called when the sun heats water and it turns into a gas?
explain: Evaporation is when liquid water warms up and changes into water vapor.

q: What is the difference between weather and climate?
options: They mean exactly the same thing
Weather is right now, while climate is the usual pattern over many years
explain: Weather is the short-term conditions, and climate is the long-term average pattern.

q: Clouds form when water vapor in the air cools and turns back into tiny droplets during what?
explain: Condensation is when water vapor cools and becomes liquid droplets, forming clouds.

q: Which type of cloud is puffy, white, and looks like cotton on a nice day?
explain: Cumulus clouds are the puffy, cotton-like clouds seen on fair days.

q: Earth's seasons are mainly caused by what?
options: The tilt of Earth's axis as it orbits the sun
The sun turning off at night
explain: Earth is tilted, so different parts get more direct sunlight at different times of year.

q: Which layer of gases surrounds Earth and holds the air we breathe and our weather?
explain: The atmosphere is the blanket of gases around Earth where weather happens.

q: Warm air does what compared to cool air, which helps create wind and weather?
options: It sinks straight to the ground
It rises because it is lighter
explain: Warm air is lighter than cool air, so it rises and helps set air moving.

q: What natural material is made of tiny bits of broken rock and helps plants grow?
explain: Soil is a mix of tiny rock pieces and other matter that plants grow in.

q: Which of these is a natural resource that comes from the Earth?
explain: Water is a natural resource we get from the Earth and need to live.

q: What do we call using items again instead of throwing them away, like cans and paper?
explain: Recycling turns used materials into new products and saves resources.

q: A mountain that can erupt with hot melted rock is called a what?
explain: A volcano is an opening in Earth where melted rock can erupt to the surface.

q: Rocks that form from cooled, hardened melted rock are called what type?
explain: Igneous rocks form when melted rock cools and hardens.

q: The slow wearing away of rock and soil by wind and water is called what?
explain: Erosion is when wind or water slowly carries away bits of rock and soil.

q: Which of these energy sources is renewable, meaning it will not run out?
explain: Sunlight is renewable because the sun keeps providing energy every day.

q: Preserved remains or prints of ancient living things found in rock are called what?
explain: Fossils are traces of ancient life saved in rock over a very long time.

q: Sedimentary rocks form when layers of sediment press together over time; which rock is sedimentary?
explain: Sandstone forms from layers of sand pressed together, making it sedimentary.

q: Earthquakes happen when large pieces of Earth's crust, called plates, do what?
options: Suddenly move or slip past each other
explain: Earthquakes are the shaking caused when Earth's plates suddenly shift.

q: Which of these is a nonrenewable resource that can run out if we use too much?
explain: Coal is nonrenewable because it takes millions of years to form and cannot be quickly replaced.

q: Which object in the sky gives Earth most of its light and heat during the day?
explain: The Sun is our closest star, and its energy gives us the light and warmth we feel every day.

q: What do we call the round object that orbits Earth and seems to change shape in the sky each month?
explain: The Moon orbits Earth, and we see it appear to change shape as it goes through its phases.

q: How many planets are in our solar system?
explain: Our solar system has eight planets, from Mercury closest to the Sun out to Neptune.

q: What makes Earth have daytime and nighttime?
options: The Sun turns off at night
explain: Earth spins once about every 24 hours, so the side facing the Sun has day while the other side has night.

q: What causes the different phases of the Moon that we see from Earth?
options: Earth's shadow always covers part of it
We see different amounts of its sunlit half
explain: As the Moon orbits Earth, we see different portions of its sunlit half, which creates the phases.

q: Which planet is often called the 'Red Planet'?
explain: Mars looks reddish because its surface is covered with iron-rich dust that acts a bit like rust.

q: Which force keeps the planets moving in their orbits around the Sun?
explain: The Sun's gravity pulls on the planets and keeps them traveling along their orbits.

q: What is the main job of a telescope?
options: To make far-away objects look larger and clearer
To send radio signals to space
explain: A telescope gathers light so distant objects like stars and planets look bigger and brighter to us.

q: What is the main reason Earth has different seasons?
options: Earth's changing distance from the Sun
explain: Earth is tilted on its axis, so different parts of the planet get more direct sunlight at different times of the year.

q: What is the Sun mostly made of?
explain: The Sun is a giant ball of hot gas made mostly of hydrogen and helium.

q: What does a light-year measure?
options: How long a year lasts on a planet
The distance light travels in one year
explain: A light-year is a distance equal to how far light travels in one year, which is a very long way.

q: Why does an astronaut appear to float inside a spaceship that is orbiting Earth?
options: There is no gravity at all in space
They are constantly falling around Earth while moving forward
Their suits are filled with helium
explain: The spaceship and astronaut both fall toward Earth while speeding forward, so they keep falling around it and feel weightless.

q: What is usually the first step engineers take when solving a problem?
options: Build the final product right away
Understand and clearly define the problem
explain: Engineers start by understanding the problem so they know exactly what their design needs to solve.

q: What is a prototype?
options: A finished product ready for sale
A drawing that can never change
An early test version of a design
explain: A prototype is an early model engineers build to test their ideas before making the final version.

q: Which shape is often used in bridges and towers because it is very strong?
explain: Triangles hold their shape well under pressure, which makes structures built with them very sturdy.

q: What do we call a person who designs and builds things to solve problems?
explain: Engineers use science and math to design and build solutions to everyday problems.

q: In the engineering design process, what should you do after testing a prototype?
options: Use what you learned to improve the design
explain: Testing shows what works and what does not, so engineers use that information to make the design better.

q: Why do engineers test their designs many times?
options: Because a rule says they must
To find and fix problems before the final build
explain: Repeated testing helps engineers catch problems early and improve the design before it is finished.

q: Which material would an engineer most likely choose to build a bridge that must hold heavy trucks?
explain: Steel is very strong and can support heavy loads, which makes it a good choice for big bridges.

q: What is a robot?
options: A machine that can be programmed to do tasks
explain: A robot is a machine that follows programmed instructions to carry out tasks, sometimes on its own.

q: Why do many bridges and tall buildings include small gaps called expansion joints?
options: To let the materials expand and shrink with temperature changes
explain: Materials grow slightly when hot and shrink when cold, and the gaps give them room to move without cracking.

q: What does it mean to 'iterate' during the engineering design process?
options: To copy someone else's finished work
To repeat and improve a design based on test results
To finish the design in only one try
explain: Iterating means going through the design steps again to make each version better than the last.

q: Which design would most likely stay stable in strong wind?
options: A tall, narrow tower with a tiny base
A wide base that gets narrower toward the top
explain: A wide, low base spreads out the weight and helps keep a structure from tipping over.

q: Why do engineers often build models or run computer simulations before making a real product?
options: Because real materials do not exist
To test ideas safely and cheaply before spending on the real thing
So they never have to test at all
explain: Models and simulations let engineers try out ideas and spot problems without the cost or risk of building the real thing first.

q: A computer mouse is an example of what kind of device?
explain: A mouse sends information into the computer when you move and click it, so it is an input device.

q: Which device is an output that shows you pictures and words?
explain: A monitor displays the computer's output so you can see images, text, and videos.

q: Which of these is a good rule for staying safe online?
options: Share your home address with strangers
Click on every pop-up you see
Post your school's name to new online friends
explain: Keeping passwords private helps protect your accounts and personal information from people who should not have them.

q: What do we call a clear set of step-by-step instructions for completing a task?
explain: An algorithm is a list of ordered steps that tells a computer or person exactly how to do something.

q: In coding, what does a 'loop' do?
options: Changes the screen color one time
explain: A loop lets a program run the same instructions over and over without writing them out each time.

q: Why does the order of steps matter in a computer program?
options: It does not matter at all
Computers follow the steps in sequence, so the order changes the result
explain: Computers carry out instructions in the exact order given, so changing the order can change what happens.

q: What is the internet?
options: A single computer in one building
A huge network that connects computers around the world
A program you buy only once
explain: The internet is a giant network linking millions of computers so they can share information worldwide.

q: What is a sensor on a device used for?
options: To detect information like light, heat, or motion
explain: A sensor detects things in the world, such as light or movement, and turns them into signals the device can use.

q: What does a computer's CPU mainly do?
options: Carries out instructions and does calculations
explain: The CPU is like the computer's brain, following instructions and doing the calculations that make programs work.

q: Why is it smart to use a strong, unique password for important accounts?
options: So it is easier to share
To make it harder for others to guess and get into your account
explain: A strong, unique password is hard to guess, which helps keep your accounts and information secure.

q: What is the difference between hardware and software?
options: Hardware is the physical parts, and software is the programs and instructions
They are exactly the same thing
Hardware is on the internet and software is not
Software is the physical parts you can touch
explain: Hardware is the equipment you can touch, while software is the coded programs that tell the hardware what to do.

q: What does 'debugging' mean in programming?
options: Adding more bugs to a program
Finding and fixing errors in the code
explain: Debugging is the process of finding mistakes in code and fixing them so the program works correctly.

q: Which scientist is famous for describing gravity and the laws of motion?
explain: Isaac Newton described how gravity pulls objects together and wrote the famous laws of motion.

q: Jane Goodall is best known for studying which animals in the wild?
explain: Jane Goodall spent many years observing wild chimpanzees and learning about their behavior.

q: Thomas Edison helped develop a practical version of which everyday invention?
explain: Edison and his team developed a long-lasting electric light bulb that people could use in their homes.

q: Which scientist is famous for the theory of evolution by natural selection?
explain: Charles Darwin explained how living things change over many generations through natural selection.

q: Marie Curie won Nobel Prizes for her groundbreaking research on what?
explain: Marie Curie studied radioactivity and even discovered new elements, earning two Nobel Prizes.

q: Katherine Johnson was a mathematician who helped NASA do what?
options: Calculate the flight paths for spaceflights
explain: Katherine Johnson's careful calculations helped guide astronauts safely on their journeys to space and back.

q: George Washington Carver is well known for his research on which crop and its many uses?
explain: Carver found hundreds of uses for peanuts and taught farmers ways to keep their soil healthy.

q: Nikola Tesla made important contributions to which kind of electricity system?
explain: Tesla helped develop the alternating current system that carries electricity to homes and buildings today.

q: Albert Einstein is most famous for developing which idea?
explain: Einstein's theory of relativity changed how scientists understand space, time, and gravity.

q: Benjamin Franklin's famous experiments helped show that lightning is a form of what?
explain: Franklin's experiments demonstrated that lightning is a powerful natural form of electricity.

q: Rosalind Franklin's X-ray images were key to discovering the structure of which molecule?
explain: Rosalind Franklin's X-ray photographs helped reveal that DNA has a twisted, double-helix shape.

q: Galileo Galilei improved the telescope and used it to observe what?
explain: Galileo used his improved telescope to discover four moons orbiting the planet Jupiter.

q: Which tool would you use to measure how long a pencil is?
explain: A ruler is marked with units of length, making it the right tool for measuring how long something is.

q: Which tool is used to measure temperature?
explain: A thermometer measures how hot or cold something is.

q: In the metric system, which unit is used to measure length?
explain: The meter is the basic metric unit for measuring length or distance.

q: What is one of the first things scientists do to start learning about something?
explain: Careful observations help scientists gather information and ask good questions about what they notice.

q: What is a hypothesis?
options: The final result of an experiment
A testable guess or prediction made before an experiment
explain: A hypothesis is an educated guess that a scientist can test with an experiment.

q: Which tool would you use to measure the volume of a liquid?
explain: A graduated cylinder has marked lines that show how much liquid it holds.

q: To keep an experiment fair, what should you do with the variables?
options: Change only one thing and keep the others the same
Change the results to fit your guess
explain: Changing just one variable at a time lets you know that it caused any difference you see.

q: What is the difference between an observation and an inference?
options: They are exactly the same thing
An inference uses only your eyes
An observation is something you sense directly, while an inference is a conclusion you reason out
An observation is always a guess
explain: You make an observation using your senses, and you make an inference by using those observations to reach a conclusion.

q: In a fair test, what is the variable you change on purpose called?
explain: The independent variable is the one thing the experimenter deliberately changes to see its effect.

q: What is the main purpose of a control group in an experiment?
options: To make the experiment take longer
To provide a comparison that stays unchanged
explain: A control group is left unchanged so scientists can compare it to the group they are testing.

q: Which metric unit would best measure the mass of an apple?
explain: Mass is measured in grams and kilograms, so grams work well for something as light as an apple.

q: What is a conclusion in the scientific method?
options: A guess made before the experiment
A statement of what the results show
A list of the tools you used
explain: A conclusion sums up what the experiment's results tell you and whether they support your hypothesis.
